tool_blocks.output:
1:6a1|//! Bootstrap secret keys for the net_crackpipe network.
2:0bd|//!
3:0d8|//! This module contains hardcoded bootstrap secret keys used to establish
4:f6d|//! the initial bootstrap nodes for the net_crackpipe network. These keys
5:5db|//! are used to derive the bootstrap node identities that clients can connect
6:a37|//! to for initial network discovery.
7:0bd|//!
8:1e6|//! The keys are hardcoded to ensure all clients connect to the same
9:a04|//! bootstrap nodes on first connection, enabling network bootstrapping.
10:d05|
11:ec5|/// Hardcoded bootstrap secret keys for the net_crackpipe bootstrap nodes.
12:611|///
13:712|/// This array contains 5 Ed25519 secret keys (32 bytes each) that correspond
14:f4c|/// to the 5 hardcoded bootstrap nodes. These keys are used to derive the
15:736|/// corresponding NodeIds that clients use for initial network bootstrap.
16:611|///
17:672|/// The keys are hardcoded to ensure all clients bootstrap from the same
18:9da|/// set of bootstrap nodes. In a production deployment, these would typically
19:acf|/// be replaced with keys controlled by the network operators.
20:611|///
21:2fa|/// # Security
22:611|///
23:8fc|/// These are public bootstrap keys - they are not secret in the traditional
24:8d0|/// sense since their corresponding public keys (NodeIds) are publicly known
25:f6e|/// and distributed with the client software. The "secret" naming is a
26:28d|/// convention indicating they are the private half of the bootstrap node
27:131|/// identity keypairs.
28:611|///
29:771|/// # Example
30:611|///
31:54a|/// ```
32:9ef|/// use net_crackpipe::_bootstrap_keys::BOOTSTRAP_SECRET_KEYS;
33:611|///
34:799|/// // Get the first bootstrap node's secret key
35:bc9|/// let first_key = &BOOTSTRAP_SECRET_KEYS[0];
36:b18|/// assert_eq!(first_key.len(), 32);
37:54a|/// ```
38:14a|pub const BOOTSTRAP_SECRET_KEYS: [[u8; 32]; 5] = [
39:34f|    [
40:3a9|        165, 48, 79, 186, 71, 176, 49, 168, 36, 74, 114, 142, 97, 104, 107, 16, 184, 0, 60, 202,
41:b2b|        123, 219, 106, 248, 177, 56, 160, 133, 88, 163, 64, 66,
42:34f|    ],
43:34f|    [
44:e35|        92, 203, 25, 13, 168, 94, 65, 89, 32, 183, 156, 237, 101, 97, 252, 62, 29, 190, 140, 35,
45:ce3|        106, 11, 86, 43, 166, 8, 250, 253, 5, 242, 239, 53,
46:34f|    ],
47:34f|    [
48:bf5|        118, 39, 26, 78, 19, 125, 210, 50, 4, 229, 108, 57, 87, 122, 16, 246, 116, 162, 16, 9, 211,
49:baa|        224, 133, 30, 66, 91, 131, 208, 209, 102, 151, 125,
50:34f|    ],
51:34f|    [
52:d7a|        183, 194, 145, 8, 164, 192, 176, 214, 186, 1, 11, 149, 45, 204, 235, 178, 201, 16, 24, 83,
53:afa|        228, 54, 232, 56, 237, 187, 153, 66, 227, 204, 228, 254,
54:34f|    ],
55:34f|    [
56:7bb|        227, 138, 221, 233, 143, 189, 36, 125, 49, 135, 151, 54, 216, 218, 196, 30, 62, 129, 22,
57:02d|        124, 71, 128, 100, 40, 215, 243, 5, 200, 44, 136, 6, 96,

turns.thinking:
Now I can see the comments. There are two types of comments:
1. Module-level doc comments (lines 1-10) - the `//!` comments
2. Item-level doc comments (lines 11-37) - the `///` comments

The user wants me to make both types shorter - max one paragraph each. Let me edit them to be shorter.

set_line.new_text: //! Bootstrap secret keys for net_crackpipe bootstrap nodes.
/// Hardcoded Ed25519 secret keys (32 bytes each) for 5 bootstrap nodes.
/// These are public bootstrap keys — their NodeIds are public; "secret" is a convention.
